title: How to Perform a TCP Check with Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring

blocks.content:
In this interactive guide you'll learn how to set up and run a TCP check in Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring. You will:

- Create and configure a Synthetic Monitoring TCP check 
- Set a query/response pair and timeout for accurate failure detection
- Select probe locations for global monitoring coverage
- Set up alerting so you're notified when a connection fails

### **Why Use TCP Checks?**

- **Monitor non-HTTP services** – Test databases, mail servers, FTP servers, and message queues at the transport layer
- **Detect connection failures early** – Catch TCP-level issues before they impact end users
- **Global visibility** – Validate connectivity and response times from probe locations around the world

## Section 1: Navigate to Synthetic Monitoring

Let's start by navigating to the Testing & Synthetics section in Grafana Cloud. This is where you create and manage all your synthetic checks.
In the main menu, navigate to **Testing & Synthetics**.
Click **Synthetic Monitoring** to open the Synthetics home page.
Click **Create new check** to get started.
Select **API Endpoint** as the check type. This is where you'll find HTTP, Ping, DNS, TCP, and Traceroute checks.

## Section 2: Configure the TCP Check

Now let's configure the TCP check. You'll give the check a name, set the request type to TCP, and specify the target host and port you want to monitor.
Enter a **Job Name** for your check, we will use **Example:TCP**.
Set the **Request Type** to **TCP**.
Enter your **Request Target** — the hostname and port to connect to, for example **smtp.gmail.com:587**.

## Section 3: Optional Parameters

In this section you can configure a query/response pair to validate the TCP handshake, set a timeout, and add labels to organize your check.
Click **Uptime** to move to the next configuration section.
Optionally click **Add query/response** to validate the TCP handshake. For example, set the query to `EHLO prober\r` and the response to `^220 ([^ ]+) ESMTP (.+)$` to verify an SMTP server greeting.
Optionally set a **Timeout**. If the check takes longer than this to run it will be marked as failed. A value of **10 seconds** is suitable for most services.
Move to the **Labels** section.
Optionally add **Labels** to help organize and filter your checks.

## Section 4: Select Probe Locations and Frequency

Probes are monitoring agents distributed around the world that connect to your target over TCP. Selecting multiple locations gives you a global view of your service's availability and response time.
Click **Execution** to move to the probe configuration section.
Select one or more **Probe Locations** to run the TCP check from. Choose locations closest to your users or infrastructure to best reflect their experience.
Optionally adjust the **Probe Frequency**. The default of 1 minute is suitable for most checks.

## Section 5: Configure Alerting

Alerting ensures you're notified as soon as a TCP connection starts failing. Set a threshold that reflects your service's tolerance for connectivity issues.
Click **Alerting** to move to the alerting configuration section.
Enable the **Probe Failed Executions** alert by clicking the checkbox.
Optionally click **Test** to validate the TCP check before saving.
Click **Save** to create your TCP check. Grafana Cloud will begin executing it immediately.

## 🎉 Congratulations!

You've successfully set up a TCP check in Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring. You can now:

- View results in the out-of-the-box **Synthetic Monitoring dashboard**, showing uptime, reachability, average latency, and SSL chain expiry
- Monitor **response latency by probe location** for deeper visibility into regional performance
- Receive **alerts** the moment TCP connections start failing

To learn more, explore other check types such as HTTP, Ping, and DNS checks — or set up private probes to monitor internal services not accessible from the public internet.
